did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning (ML) for automated skin lesion diagnosis. Specifically, it employs a hybrid approach combining Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) for preprocessing, hybrid orthogonal combination of local binary patterns (OC-LBP) for feature extraction, ReliefF for feature selection, and ensemble Extra Tree (ET) classifiers to classify eight types of skin lesions using the ISIC 2019 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is labeled as an 'Original Article' and presents a novel hybrid machine learning framework for skin lesion diagnosis. It details specific methods (GMM, OC-LBP, ReliefF, ensemble Extra Tree), describes the experimental setup using the ISIC 2019 dataset, and reports original results (97.31% accuracy). It is not a review or survey of prior literature.

title: Improving Skin Lesion Diagnosis: A Hybrid Approach Using Orthogonal Combination of Local Binary Pattern Features and Ensemble Learning for Diagnostic Accuracy
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of automated skin lesion diagnosis by proposing a hybrid machine learning framework to improve classification accuracy. The method utilizes the ISIC 2019 dataset, employing a Gaussian Mixture Model for artifact removal and hybrid orthogonal combination of local binary patterns for feature extraction. To handle class imbalance, random oversampling is applied, followed by ReliefF feature selection. The framework evaluates multiple classifiers, ultimately identifying an ensemble Extra Tree model as the most effective. The proposed approach achieves a superior accuracy of 97.31%, demonstrating its potential for enhancing early skin cancer detection.

all_task_types.target: Eight different types of skin lesions (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevus, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Vascular Lesions)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin lesions using the ISIC 2019 dataset. The study evaluates traditional ML classifiers (Ensemble Extra Tree, KNN, DT, RF, MLP) to predict discrete diagnostic categories. The primary model (Ensemble Extra Tree) achieved 97.31% accuracy. The task is explicitly defined as classification in the keywords and abstract.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2019 Challenge dataset, a publicly available benchmark containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions. It is noted for having visual inconsistencies, class imbalance, and artifacts such as hair occlusion, rulers, markers, and uneven lighting.

use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) using the 10-fold cross-validation results.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to mitigate class imbalance by randomly duplicating samples from minority classes to match the number of instances in the majority class, creating a more balanced dataset prior to model training.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid machine learning framework, combining Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) for artifact removal, hybrid orthogonal combination of local binary patterns (OC-LBP) for feature extraction, ReliefF for feature selection, and an ensemble Extra Tree (ET) classifier, effectively improves automated skin lesion diagnosis.

The ensemble Extra Tree (ET) classifier achieved superior performance with an accuracy of 97.31% on the balanced ISIC 2019 dataset using 10-fold cross-validation, outperforming KNN, Decision Tree, Random Forest, and Multilayer Perceptron classifiers.

The hybrid OC-LBP method with radii (2, 5) provided an optimal trade-off between capturing fine-grained local textures and broader global structures, balancing feature distinctiveness and generalization while mitigating noise interference.
Random oversampling was necessary to mitigate class imbalance in the ISIC 2019 dataset; without it, model performance dropped significantly (e.g., Hybrid OC-LBP + ET accuracy fell to 57.81%).

The model demonstrated high accuracy for most lesion types (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Vascular Lesions, Benign Keratosis), but Nevus (NV) classification was more challenging (78.1% accuracy) due to textural and color similarities with benign keratosis, BCC, and early-stage Melanoma.

The study advances early skin lesion diagnosis and enhances patient care in dermatology by providing a non-invasive, efficient, and accurate diagnostic tool that reduces reliance on subjective visual inspection and inter-observer variability.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2019 dataset, which is known for visual inconsistencies, class imbalance, and artifacts (hair, rulers, markers), limiting external validation and generalizability to other clinical settings.

Random oversampling was applied to mitigate class imbalance; without it, the Ensemble Extra Tree accuracy dropped significantly to 57.81%, indicating high sensitivity to data distribution and potential overfitting to minority classes.

The model struggled with Nevus (NV) classification (78.1% accuracy) due to textural and color similarities with benign keratosis, BCC, and early-stage Melanoma, highlighting a specific diagnostic ambiguity in the dataset.
No formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported; statistical significance of performance differences between models was not established.

The study uses traditional machine learning (GMM, OC-LBP, ReliefF, ET) rather than deep learning, which may offer lower computational overhead but potentially less feature representation power compared to state-of-the-art CNNs or Transformers.
Ethical approval and participant consent were not obtained by the authors; they relied on the publicly available ISIC 2019 dataset where ethical procedures were handled by the original investigators.

The paper does not report per-class metrics (precision, recall, F1) for the balanced dataset, only overall accuracy and log loss, making it difficult to assess performance on specific lesion types beyond the noted NV challenge.

The preprocessing step using GMM removes artifacts without cropping, which preserves image content but may introduce noise or incomplete removal if the model parameters are not robust across diverse artifact types.
